metadata.title: Framing Climate Change in the 5<sup>th</sup> Estate: Comparing Online Advocacy and Denial Webpages and Their Engagement

abstract: Debates about anthropogenic climate change grew increasingly polarized as online channels emerged as primary news sources. This raises the question of how online media shape the perceived salience of climate change issues. Guided by agenda-setting theory and framing theory, this study utilized topic modeling to examine online climate change advocacy and denial webpages posted from 2007-2019. Engagement with media agendas, public agendas, and framing related to climate change were examined. Advocacy webpages were more engaging than denial webpages. The more frequently that a climate change topical frame was covered by online media, the more likely it would be engaged on social media (SM). Climate change topical frames differed in their ability to engage SM users. Several competing climate change advocacy and denial topical frames differed significantly in SM engagement. Results can help researchers to design effective climate change campaigns as well as develop programs to track and combat online misinformation.

sections.paper_id: Xu and Atkin - 2022 - Framing Climate Change in the 5th Estate Comparing Online Advocacy and Denial Webpages a
paragraphs: The emergence of online channels as primary news sources has intensified debates on anthropogenic climate change, which remain polarizing, despite enjoying universal support among scientists (e.g., Collins and Nerlich, 2015;Matthews, 2015). This raises the question of how online media shape the perceived salience of climate change issues. Digital media clearly alter how individuals communicate about climate change, notably how the rise in climate change skepticism has been enabled by online spaces (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019;Walter et al., 2018). An analysis of several million postings found that 25% of climate crisis tweets came from bots; in particular "…bots are not just prevalent, but disproportionately so in topics that were supportive of Trump's announcement or skeptical of climate science and action" (Millman, 2020, para 6). Although this research suggests an influence of mechanized bots in amplifying denialist messages, our understanding of online climate change message influences remains incomplete.

Past work indicates a lack of correspondence between scientific information and interpretations made by the media (e.g., Brownell et al., 2013). However, climate scientists and scientific institutions rarely engage with online debates about climate change (Brownell et al., 2013;Schäfer, 2012). Instead, online media represent climate data to favor selected views and engage a large number of SM users (Brownell et al., 2013;Schäfer, 2012). As many users lack scientific literacy, their climate knowledge tends to be shaped by the media (Carmichael & Brulle, 2017). This raises the question of how media reporting-including that offered by emerging digital media-shapes the perceived salience of climate change issues. Guided by agenda-setting theory and framing theory, the present study utilizes computational methods to examine engagement with online media agendas, public agendas, and framing related to climate change.

Xu and Atkin - 2022 - Framing Climate Change in the 5th Estate Comparing Online Advocacy and Denial Webpages a
paragraphs: Extensive research documents how the media and opinion leaders set the agenda and frame climate change, which may encourage climate denial beliefs and discussions. The notion of agenda-setting was first introduced by McCombs and Shaw (1972) to explain the positive relationship between media exposure and attitude change. In other words, the more frequently the media cover an issue, the more likely the audience will perceive the issue as important. Thus, media agendas often mirror public agendas. To wit, when the media repeatedly discuss the economic impacts of climate change, the audience should perceive it as a more salient issue. In the digital era, SM users who regard an issue as critical engage in related discussions by reacting, commenting, and sharing original content (Wu & Atkin, 2017).

Several influences contribute to the media agenda, such as gatekeepers, organizations, and cultural or ideological considerations. Even though some individualssuch as politicians, celebrities, and elites-have the ability to set the agenda, input from the general public has not been regarded as an important factor (Shoemaker & Reese, 2014). With the development of the Internet and SM, however, determining who sets the public agenda has become a complicated question. Any SM user can start a new discussion as well as react and respond to an existing discussion. In the climate change domain, for instance, Lewandowsky, Cook, Fay, and Gignac (2019) found that beliefs about climate change "are partially shaped by readers' perception of how widely an opinion expressed in a blog post appears to be shared by other readers" (p. 1445).

sections.paper_id: Xu and Atkin - 2022 - Framing Climate Change in the 5th Estate Comparing Online Advocacy and Denial Webpages a
sections.header_raw: Agenda Setting and Climate Change in the Online Sphere
sections.header_norm: agenda setting and climate change in the online sphere
paragraphs: Discussion of climate change and engagement with said topics online are notably increasing (Nisbet, 2009;Schäfer, 2012). Broadly speaking, exposure to such information can influence public perceptions and salience concerning environmental issues, including climate change (e.g., Lewandowsky et al., 2019). A national survey by Pew (2020), for instance, reveals a striking rise in environmental protection and global climate change atop public agendas in recent years; they conclude that "nearly as many Americans say protecting the environment should be a top policy priority (64%) as say this about strengthening the economy (67%)" (p. 1).

Online engagement refers to the context-dependent, collective experiences that individuals have when consuming media as well as the ability to react to, comment, and share information. Here, a higher level of engagement is defined by the level at which information is consumed, such as being reacted to, commented on, and/or shared (Calder et al., 2016). Since engagement represents active information processing, deep learning, and comprehension, it can play a critical role in the persuasion process; engagement with SM can be utilized to persuade and may facilitate behavioral change (e.g., Neiger et al., 2013). Reviewing the behavioral stages of Twitter-based climate change movements like the 21st Conference of the Parties (COP21), Fernandez et al.(2016) found that engagement through communication was important for individuals to take on climate changebased behavior changes. The present framework assumes that these emerging digital media outlets present at least as many affordances for engagement, relative to the legacy media modalities profiled in McCombs and Shaw's (1972) seminal study (see Groshek & Groshek, 2013). Regarding specific engagement processes, exposure to COP21 information online encourages participation in climate change discourse online, specifically blogs and social network sites (Arlt et al., 2018).

Theoretically, when an issue receives significant attention and discussion on SM, users who participate in the discussion set the public agenda and can even influence the media agenda (Neuman et al., 2014), which is regarded as reverse agenda-setting.

Reverse agenda-setting takes place when the power of setting the public agenda has been transferred from the media to the general public and the media has to respond to perceived public opinion (McCombs, 2004). Given the affordances in two-way communication presented by digital modalities-which can counter the gate-keeping power of traditional media-a focus on reverse agenda setting is warranted. Groshek and Groshek (2013), for instance, underscore the importance of considering SM in intermedia agenda-setting processes; their own results suggest that social networking sites can set traditional media agendas. Based on the research and theory reviewed above, then, we predict that the media set the agenda regarding climate change. Climate change-related topics that receive more media exposure are more likely to be perceived as important and are more likely to engage the audience on SM.

This overarching trend can be explicated across several content subdomains that can be tracked in the data. In particular, drawing from themes emerging in prior research, this dynamic should be evident across a wide range of content frames (see Arlt, et al., 2018), encompassing such domains as political movements, interpretations of data, natural processes, or impacts on particular regions. Although specific arguments and examples may vary across climate change adherents and skeptics, the current framework assumes that (1) frames can be grouped across common themes, and (2) dynamics encouraging greater engagement can be explicated across all content categories. This reinforces the need to examine frames supporting the notion of climate change (i.e., "advocacy frames") and those that are skeptical of it (i.e., "denial frames") respectively. The current framework thus posits that greater discussion of climate change issues drives SM engagement across variegated issue frames over time. More formally, the following guiding hypotheses are offered to explicate the expected increases in engagement with climate change frames as SM gained prominence after 2006: H1: The more frequently climate change advocacy frames are covered by online media, the more likely the frames will be engaged on social media. H2: The more frequently climate change denial frames are covered by online media, the more likely the frames will be engaged on social media.

Framing and Counter-Framing in the Online Sphere Agenda-setting studies have increasingly focused on second-level agenda-setting or framing, which examines how an issue is constructed by the media and its audiences (McCombs, 2004). A frame reveals the relationship between two concepts (Scheufele & Tewksbury, 2007). Nisbet (2009) cites the example of Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist E. O. Wilson, who associated climate change with religious and moral concerns in order to persuade religious leaders that it's related to their faith (Nisbet, 2009).

Previous studies have reviewed commonly used frames in climate change discussions (Nisbet, 2009;Schäfer and O'Neill, 2017). For instance, climate change is often framed as an issue overlapping with other economic and social issues, such as energy, security, health, etc. (Lakoff, 2010). News media often portray climate change within a framework of uncertainty, reflexivity, or controversy, and utilize emotional imagery to captivate and convince the audience, whether the viewpoint is one of acceptance or skepticism (e.g., Olausson, 2011). However, many studies on framing have largely focused on legacy media, which are characterized by different attributes than their digital media counterparts. Digital media, for instance, possess advantages in capacity, interactivity, and selectivity (including long-tail micro-targeting), which sometimes result in misinformation and pseudoscience appearing to be more engaging than scientific information (Xu, 2019). This may stem from the novelty appeal of denial articles-and greater affirmation sought by their adherents-amidst the perceived orthodoxy of climate change science advocacy dominating media and academic institutions (e.g., Millman, 2020).

Opinion leaders adopt different frames to support their positions. Ideally, the public will understand, evaluate, and discuss the issue in the same direction of the given frames. However, in reality, opposing sides will create counter-frames, which discuss the relationship between the same two concepts, but frame the relationship in a different direction (Chong & Druckman, 2013). For instance, climate change deniers argue that action on climate change will lead to negative economic consequences. At the same time, climate advocates state that action on climate change can foster a sustainable economy (Nisbet, 2009). Competing frames allow the public to assess the arguments in different positions using their knowledge, prior beliefs, experience, etc. This begs the question about which of the two competing frames is more engaging, persuasive or effective in the online sphere. The answers to such questions, in turn, can help us obtain clues about which side is more supported by the public (Chong & Druckman, 2013). According to the Pew Research Center, more than half of the U.S. adults do not believe in climate change or do not believe that human activity causes climate change (Funk & Kennedy, 2016) and misinformation may be more engaging than scientific information, especially on the Internet (Xu, 2019). Based on this rationale, we posit that: H3a: Online climate change denial webpages are more engaging than advocacy webpages.
H3b: A climate change denial frame is more engaging in the online sphere than its advocacy counter-frame.

Given the paucity of research addressing the relative popularity of environmental issues, past work provides little guide for conceptualizing such processes in the online sphere. It's useful, then, to inquire which of these frames are most pervasive and which are more engaging than others online. More formally: RQ1: What are the most (a) frequently used and (b) engaging frames in online climate change advocacy and denial webpages?

Even though framing cannot be avoided in the communication process (Nisbet, 2009), its effect does not last long. An experimental study discovered that the framing effect disappeared after 10 days (Druckman & Nelson, 2003). Thus, in order to continue engaging and persuading the audience, media agents may need to repeatedly use the same frame or apply new frames in their messages. A handful studies have aimed to explore the dynamics of frames over time (e.g., Neuman et al., 2014;Tewksbury and Scheufele, 2019). However, to our knowledge, prior studies have yet to investigate the dynamics of climate change frames and their effects over time. It's useful, then, to pose the following question: RQ2: How have climate change advocacy and denial frames evolved during the social media era?

Wilson, who associated climate change with religious and moral concerns in order to persuade religious leaders that it's related to their faith (Nisbet, 2009).

Previous studies have reviewed commonly used frames in climate change discussions (Nisbet, 2009;Schäfer and O'Neill, 2017). For instance, climate change is often framed as an issue overlapping with other economic and social issues, such as energy, security, health, etc. (Lakoff, 2010). News media often portray climate change within a framework of uncertainty, reflexivity, or controversy, and utilize emotional imagery to captivate and convince the audience, whether the viewpoint is one of acceptance or skepticism (e.g., Olausson, 2011). However, many studies on framing have largely focused on legacy media, which are characterized by different attributes than their digital media counterparts. Digital media, for instance, possess advantages in capacity, interactivity, and selectivity (including long-tail micro-targeting), which sometimes result in misinformation and pseudoscience appearing to be more engaging than scientific information (Xu, 2019). This may stem from the novelty appeal of denial articles-and greater affirmation sought by their adherents-amidst the perceived orthodoxy of climate change science advocacy dominating media and academic institutions (e.g., Millman, 2020).

Opinion leaders adopt different frames to support their positions. Ideally, the public will understand, evaluate, and discuss the issue in the same direction of the given frames. However, in reality, opposing sides will create counter-frames, which discuss the relationship between the same two concepts, but frame the relationship in a different direction (Chong & Druckman, 2013). For instance, climate change deniers argue that action on climate change will lead to negative economic consequences. At the same time, climate advocates state that action on climate change can foster a sustainable economy (Nisbet, 2009). Competing frames allow the public to assess the arguments in different positions using their knowledge, prior beliefs, experience, etc. This begs the question about which of the two competing frames is more engaging, persuasive or effective in the online sphere. The answers to such questions, in turn, can help us obtain clues about which side is more supported by the public (Chong & Druckman, 2013). According to the Pew Research Center, more than half of the U.S. adults do not believe in climate change or do not believe that human activity causes climate change (Funk & Kennedy, 2016) and misinformation may be more engaging than scientific information, especially on the Internet (Xu, 2019). Based on this rationale, we posit that: H3a: Online climate change denial webpages are more engaging than advocacy webpages.

H3b: A climate change denial frame is more engaging in the online sphere than its advocacy counter-frame.

Given the paucity of research addressing the relative popularity of environmental issues, past work provides little guide for conceptualizing such processes in the online sphere. It's useful, then, to inquire which of these frames are most pervasive and which are more engaging than others online. More formally: RQ1: What are the most (a) frequently used and (b) engaging frames in online climate change advocacy and denial webpages?

Even though framing cannot be avoided in the communication process (Nisbet, 2009), its effect does not last long. An experimental study discovered that the framing effect disappeared after 10 days (Druckman & Nelson, 2003). Thus, in order to continue engaging and persuading the audience, media agents may need to repeatedly use the same frame or apply new frames in their messages. A handful studies have aimed to explore the dynamics of frames over time (e.g., Neuman et al., 2014;Tewksbury and Scheufele, 2019). However, to our knowledge, prior studies have yet to investigate the dynamics of climate change frames and their effects over time. It's useful, then, to pose the following question: RQ2: How have climate change advocacy and denial frames evolved during the social media era?
sections.preview_head: Discussion of climate change and engagement with said topics online are notably increasing (Nisbet, 2009;Schäfer, 2012). Broadly speaking, exposure to such information can influence public perceptions and salience concerning environmental issues, including climate change (e.g., Lewandowsky et al., 2019). A national survey by Pew (2020), for instance, reveals a striking rise in environmental protection and global climate change atop public agendas in recent years; they conclude that "nearly as many Americans say protecting the environment should be a top policy priority (64%) as say this about strengthening the economy (67%)" (p. 1).

sections.paper_id: Xu and Atkin - 2022 - Framing Climate Change in the 5th Estate Comparing Online Advocacy and Denial Webpages a
paragraphs: People typically use search engines to look for a specific topic or issue (Jansen et al., 2008). The top three most frequently used search engines-Google, Bing, and Yahoo-account for 96% of the global market share (Johnson, 2019). Thirteen search terms were used to search in the three search engines to obtain climate change-related webpages; they include: climate change, global warm, environment change, greenhouse effect, climate variability, climate denial, climate conspiracy, climate hoax, climastrology, warmunist, climatista, global warm scam, and climate skeptic. We intentionally included terms such terms as climastrology, warmunist, and climatista-often searched by climate deniers-as identified in previous work (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019). More than 90% of searchers only look at the first page of the search results and only a small percentage will go past the third page (van Deursen & van Dijk, 2009), which imply that webpages appearing after the first few pages of the search results based on a search term are less influencing since only a few people will access them. To ensure more influencing webpages are captured, this study used Python (version 3.5.2) to capture the first 200 search results (10 pages) for each search term, using each search engine, and obtain each result's link on April 10, 2019.

After removing duplicated links, 3091 unique links' URLs were entered into an Excel sheet. Two trained coders checked whether each webpage met the five criteria to be included in the final analysis. Webpages were included if they (a) were active; (b) were written in English; (c) contained text; (d) were not listserv, newsgroup, or discussion pages; and (e) did not require additional software to download. After excluding webpages that did not meet our requirements, 2112 webpages remained. Coders then assigned each webpage into one of the three categories: advocacy, denial, or neutral (intercoder correlation = .86).

A denial webpagearguesthatthereisnoglobalwarmingnorclimatechange,humanactivitiesdonot contributetoclimatechangeorclimatevariability,orclimatechangedoesnothaveanynegativeeffects.Aneutralwebpagedoesnotstatewhetheritbelievesthatclimatechangeishappeningor human activities lead to climate change. Such webpages sometimes present both sides and sometimes are irrelevant to the climate change debate. Based on the hypothesis and research questions, the present study only analyzed webpages that advocated and deniedclimatechange.

sections.paper_id: Xu and Atkin - 2022 - Framing Climate Change in the 5th Estate Comparing Online Advocacy and Denial Webpages a
paragraphs: Engagement. The sum of shares, reactions, and comments that a webpage has received on SM has been regarded as a webpage's engagement in past work (Rus & Cameron, 2016). In the current study, engagement was measured by the total number of shares that a webpage has received from its publication date to the data retrieval date on Facebook, Twitter, Reddit, and Pinterest. These numbers were obtained using Buzzsumo, a marketing research company. Topical frame. There are different types of frames and the present study only discusses topical frames, which are related to the media content and specific to an issue. Topical frames have accounted for the bulk of past climate change framing research (Schäfer et al., 2017). In the current study, topical frames are identified by topic modeling, which applies machine learning algorithms to automatically categorize unstructured content into different topical frames based on similar patterns. Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic modeling is one of the most widely used and validated methods in topic modeling (Arun et al., 2010;Deveaud et al., 2014). Based on the Bayesian hierarchical model, LDA estimates the probabilities of a webpage belonging to each topical frame derived from the weight of each word to each topical frame, the frequency of each word in each webpage, and the discrete probability distribution that a word belongs to each topical frame (Blei et al., 2003;Blei & Lafferty, 2009).

sections.paper_id: Xu and Atkin - 2022 - Framing Climate Change in the 5th Estate Comparing Online Advocacy and Denial Webpages a
paragraphs: Climate change advocacy and denial webpages were examined using R (version 3.5.2). Stop words included in the English stop word list in the R package "tidytext", such as "the", "a", "of," were removed before analysis (Lewis et al., 2004). First, the R package "ldatuning" was used to determine the most suitable number of advocacy and denial topical frames, respectively. Deveaud et al.'s unsupervised Latent Concept Modeling method generated the optimal number of advocacy and denial topical frames, respectively, by comparing the overall dissimilarity among topical frames. The number of topical frames used to reach the maximum overall dissimilarity was regarded as the optimal number of topical frames (Deveaud et al., 2014). The information divergence was calculated for instances in which the number of topical frames fell into a range of values equaling from 2 to 60.

Information divergence reached the maximum for advocacy webpages when the number of topical frames was 16 (D = 1.94) and for denial webpages when the number of topical frames was 15 (D = 1.75). In other words, the optimal number of topical frames is 16 in climate change advocacy webpages. The optimal number of topical frames is 15 in climate change denial webpages. Next, the R package "topicmodels" generated advocacy and denial topical frames and returned the most frequently used and valuable terms in each topical frame. Based on these top terms (See Tables 1 and 2), we manually assigned each topical frame a name and randomly checked 10% of webpages (within each topical frame) to confirm that topical frame names were highly relevant. Then, the Wilcoxon Rank Sum test was used to compare the number of words and engagement among advocacy and denial webpages.

The Wilcoxon Rank Sum test was also used to compare the engagement level of climate change advocacy topical frames and their denial counter-topical frames. Spearman's rank correlation was applied to determine the correlation between the number of webpages in a topical frame and its engagement. A Kruskal-Wallis test was used to compare advocacy and denial topical frames' engagement. The significance level is p <0.05. Finally, stacked area charts and line plots were utilized to visualize climate change advocacy and denial topical frames. Again, since only 15 advocacy webpages and three denial webpages were published before 2007, in order to generate figures that can reveal the data clearly, only webpages published online from 2007 to 2019 were included in the final step.

sections.paper_id: Xu and Atkin - 2022 - Framing Climate Change in the 5th Estate Comparing Online Advocacy and Denial Webpages a
paragraphs: The final analysis included 1,581,613 words from 1,149 advocacy webpages (Median = 818) and 705,075 words from 503 denial webpages (Median = 812). A Wilcoxon Rank Sum test revealed that support and denial webpages did not differ in the number of words that they included (p = 0.39). H1 and H2 posited that media exposure to climate change advocacy and denial topical frames was related to engagement. Spearman's rank correlation analysis revealed a significant and strong correlation between the number of webpages in a climate change advocacy topical frame and the topical frame's SM engagement (r = 0.72, p = 0.002). Results also discovered a significant and strong correlation between the number of webpages in a climate change denial topical frame and the topical frame's SM engagement (r = 0.87, p < 0.001), providing support for H1 and H2.

RQ1 queried the most commonly used and engaging topical frames used in climate change advocacy and denial webpages. As shown in Table 1, the most frequently used Note. This table presented five of the most representative terms of each topical frame and the names that were assigned to each topical frame. For example, top terms in one of the climate change advocacy topical frames-ice, ocean, rise, sea, and level-indicated that this topical frame discussed sea level rise. Thus, we assigned the name, "sea level rise," to this topical frame. N webpage represents the total number of webpages belonging to a topical frame. N engagement represents the total number of shares that a topical frame has received. Median engagement represents the median number of shares that a topical frame has received.

advocacy topical frame involved a discussion of the causes of climate change, followed by public accountability, which motivated people to think carefully and believe in scientific facts and evidence. The least frequently discussed climate change advocacy topical frame involved climate variability models. Kruskal-Wallis test results revealed that the 16 support topical frames were significantly different in their abilities to engage the audience (χ 2 = 192.06, df = 15, Note. p < 0.001). Based on median engagement levels, the topical frame that discussed Trump's environmental policy was significantly more engaging than other topics, followed by sea level rise. The topical frame of environmental economics was the least engaging topic. The second least engaging topic involved the causes of climate change.

As shown in Table 2, the most frequently appearing denial topical frame was about sea level, which stated that variation in sea temperatures and levels was normal and was not related to global warming. Topical frames regarding research published by skeptic scientists' studies and lying media were also frequently used. The least frequently applied climate change denial topical frame was about positive impacts on agriculture, which discussed how warmer weather benefited agriculture.

Kruskal-Wallis test results revealed that the 15 denial topical frames significantly different in engagement (χ 2 = 34.84, df = 14, p = 0.001). Based on median engagement levels, the frame that discussed positive impacts on agriculture was significantly more engaging than other topics, followed by left-wing foundations setting the agenda. The topical frame of religious and moral concerns was the least engaging.

H3a asked if climate change denial webpages were more engaging than advocacy webpages. In total, advocacy webpages were shared 5,594,823 times online. Denial webpages received 3,806,561 shares online. Wilcoxon Rank Sum test results revealed that advocacy webpages (Median = 112) were significantly more engaging than denial webpages (Median = 75; p = 0.02). Thus, H3a was not supported.

H3b posited that climate change denial topical frames were more engaging than their advocacy counter-topical frames. Results revealed five pairs of competing climate change advocacy and denial topical frames: (1) advocacy topical frame that discussed sea level rise and its denial counter-topical frame arguing that sea level did not rise; (2) advocacy topical frame of scientific research and its denial counter-topical frame claiming that errors existed in climate change research; (3) advocacy topical frame that criticized Trump's environmental policies and its denial counter-topical frame that supported Trump's environmental policies; (4) advocacy topical frame that discussed climate change's negative impacts on agriculture and its denial counter-topical frame, stating that warmer weather benefited agriculture; (5) advocacy topical frame and its denial counter-topical frame regarding public accountability.

A Wilcoxon Rank Sum test was used to compare each pair's engagement. Advocacy and denial topical frames that discussed sea level did not differ in their engagement (p = 0.06). Climate change advocacy and denial topical frames that focused on research were not significantly different in their abilities to engage SM users (p = 0.35). A climate change advocacy topical frame that criticized Trump's environmental policies (Median = 994) was significantly more engaging than the climate change denial topical frame that praised Trump's environmental policies (Median = 16, p < 0.001). The climate change advocacy topical frame involving negative impacts on agriculture (Median = 16) was significantly less engaging than the climate change denial topical frame of positive impacts on agriculture (Median = 231, p = 0.006). A climate change advocacy topical frame involving public accountability (Median = 159) was significantly more engaging than the climate change denial topical frame of public accountability (Median = 11, p = 0.008). Thus, the posited relationship was only supported for the fourth pair of competing climate change advocacy and denial topical frames, which addressed climate change's impacts on agriculture. sections.paper_id: Xu and Atkin - 2022 - Framing Climate Change in the 5th Estate Comparing Online Advocacy and Denial Webpages a
Xu and Atkin - 2022 - Framing Climate Change in the 5th Estate Comparing Online Advocacy and Denial Webpages a
sections.header_raw: Media Agenda, Public Agenda, and Framing
sections.header_norm: media agenda public agenda and framing
paragraphs: The strong correlation between climate change topical frames and SM engagement demonstrates that the public agenda reflects the online media agenda. However, the present data cannot establish directionality between the online media agenda and the public agenda. Taken in the context of experimental work that did establish causality, then, it's useful to consider Lewandowski et al.'s (2019) work; they found, in particular, that climate change perceptions are shaped by blog readers' perception of how widely a posted opinion is shared by other readers. Working from that assumption, both climate change advocacy and denial topical frames may encourage related SM discussions. An alternative explanation is that heated SM discussions regarding certain climate change topical frames lead advocates-and skeptics-to produce more related online webpages. Later work could profitably employ time-series data to determine the causal direction in these relationships.

Underscoring the need to consider these media environment influences over time, Bennett (1990) notes that media fragmentation invites consideration of political communication effects on a more individual level. Continuing media fragmentation may challenge the content homogeneity assumptions underpinning such penitential theories as cultivation and agenda setting (e.g., Atkin et al., 2015). These perspectives were developed over a half-century ago-when most Americans received most of their information from three commercial networks and a monopoly newspaper-and convergent public opinion effects seemed more tenable. This increasingly fragmenting media environment, and attendant "scatter-streaming" of public opinion, may undermine social conceptions of shared meaning…if not truth, itself (e.g., Jeffres et al., 2001).

The highly targeted nature of digital media channels-replete with their affordances for reciprocal user access-render reverse agenda setting a particularly helpful framework to explore SM influences in setting traditional media agendas (Groshek & Groshek, 2013). That said, a significant correlation between climate change topical frames and SM engagement does not necessarily imply that frequently applied climate change topical frames effectively engage their audiences (and generate online discussion). For example, our results indicate that the most frequently used topical frame by climate advocates-discussing the causes of climate change-was one of the least engaging of all advocacy topical frames. In other words, advocacy message designers aim to educate the public on the factors that contribute to climate change by producing a large number of related online webpages. However, these webpages may not engage internet users effectively.

In contrast, topical frames with high personal relevance were more effective in engaging the public; these include topical frames related to political issues (e.g., Trump's environmental policies and Republicans' opinions) and threats to lives and health (e.g., sea level rise, fossil fuel industry, disease outbreaks, and extreme weather events). This dynamic can be explained by the notion that pure scientific knowledge has been less likely to gain public attention and interest, relative to information that is relevant to personal and political interest (Xu, 2019).

Our finding that the topical frame outlining the causes of climate change was increasingly used by climate advocates recently is intriguing. One explanation might be that message designers were not aware of the ineffectiveness of this topical frame. Climate change advocates and message designers are thus recommended to connect climate change to issues that are personally relevant to the public. The present study documents the utility of an approach that combines easy-access online data and adopts machine learning techniques to understand the dynamics of online media frames and public opinion.

Study results further demonstrate that more advocacy than denial webpages appeared in top search engine results and advocacy webpages were significantly more engaging. However, several climate change denial topical frames were engaging on SM, such as the topical frame that discussed climate change's positive effects on agriculture and the topical frame about left-wing foundations setting the agenda. Plus, the topical frame that discussed climate change's positive effects on agriculture was significantly more engaging than its advocacy counter frame. However, the fact that audience members engage in a discussion does not mean that they agree with the viewpoints presented. SM users may share a webpage just to post opposing opinions against the original perspective.

Even so, engagement is critical in the persuasion process. A powerful and welldesigned message is meaningless unless it is clicked, viewed, shared, and/or discussed (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019). A webpage that receives more shares means that more people will be exposed to it, which may lead more people to change their attitudes and behaviors. Thus, it is critical for climate advocates to design effective counter-frames to address the points discussed in engaging denial frames. This is particularly important given that a quarter of these tweets come from bots, the bulk of which express skepticism about climate science and action (Millman, 2020). Given Lewandowski et al.'s (2019) finding that climate change beliefs are shaped by reader perceptions of how widely such posts are shared by other readers, later work could profitably investigate the impact of posts, utilizing third-person, spiral of science and related perspectives.

With regard to study limitations, a webpage's engagement was measured with the total number of shares that it received. The number of reactions and comments can also be regarded as a webpage's ability to engage the audience and represents a fruitful avenue for future studies. Moreover, this study only searched 13 terms in Google, Bing, and Yahoo, which might miss other climate change online webpages. Furthermore, other social media platforms (e.g., Instagram) and other message types (e.g., video) were not included in the present study. Finally, neutral webpages were excluded from the study, but would represent a promising avenue for later engagement research.
